en.name: Governing sustainable tourism in territories with high environmental value: reconnecting tourism and nature for addressing the climate crisis with an ecosystem-based approach
en.description:
The Mediterranean is a vulnerable hotspot in the current Climate Emergency. Tourism is and will be one of the most impacted economic sectors, with heightened risks, including flooding and erosion of key coastal assets. The recent Transition Pathway for Tourism and the Glasgow Declaration are building a global momentum for Climate Action in Tourism, but policy makers and destinations need support and new approaches. Nature can be a cornerstone to address this challenge, while ensuring the protection of key destination assets, such as beaches. How? Through climate mitigation and adaptation using ecosystem-based approaches, which can only be accomplished through strong governance, robust planning, and the engagement of the private sector and citizens.
NaTour4CChange will build on successful experiences at Med and global level to test solutions for increasing the resilience of coastal destinations in the Mediterranean. The project will set common methods to allow participating regions to assess their tourism-related climate adaptation and mitigation priorities, and take climate action via plans and strategies, supported by cooperative governance. In coastal destinations, cross-sector teams will deliver specific tourism climate action plans, focusing climate adaptation, where Nature-based Solutions will be tested to ensure their feasibility, while innovative destination marketing and communication approaches will engage private stakeholders, visitors and residents in climate action. Finally, NaTour4CChange will promote cross-fertilization among participating regions and destinations, to achieve common methods and to compare the different tested plans and solutions, which will lead to lessons, best practices and policy recommendations. Associated partners and external stakeholders —at regional, national and international scale— will benefit from these learnings in order to make a significant Med contribution to this global challenge.
en.expected_achievements:
Creating the methods and sharing progress and learnings in order to design the Climate Change action planning for project’s Regions and to improve the Climate Change-related resilience of pilot tourism destinations
Governance: Improve regions capacity for integrated tourism climate policy and governance to mitigate and adapt to climate change.
To increase the Climate Change-related resilience of pilot tourism destinations through a long-term planning of evidence-based activities to adapt the destination to CC and through the testing of Nature-based Solutions
en.actual_achievements:
Natour4CChange partnership began meeting regularly online in December 2023, after the Programme's approval, to manage the contracting process and initiate project activities. These meetings were organized, coordinated, and moderated by the LP. The kick-off meeting took place via Zoom on March 5, 2024, and an in-person PSC meeting followed in Zagreb on June 17–19, 2024, where further steps for each WP were defined and training for Regions and pilot sites was also provided. 
In WP1, the first activity focused on launching Act. 1.1 (WP Coordination), resulting in the realization of Deliv. 1.1.1, i.e the Coordination Plan. Carbon footprint monitoring (Act. 1.2) is ongoing, with all partners submitting necessary data following the  Community4Tourism meeting in Rome and the June meeting in Zagreb. The first deliv., led by Plan Blue, issued from Act. 1.3, was the designing of an online survey, implementing it, and processing survey results on best practices in climate action for coastal tourism destinations. The survey aimed to identify gaps in climate resilience actions in Mediterranean tourism, and the findings will serve as a baseline for future activities.
Deliv. 1.4.1, i.e. the Methodological Framework for assessing coastal tourism-related issues concerning climate change (CC) mitigation, and Deliv. 1.4.2, focused on CC adaptation, were developed to be used by Regions and pilot destinations. These frameworks contain criteria and indicators for climate change mitigation and adaptation tailored to territorial dynamics in each region and pilot site. A training session on these methods was conducted during the June 2024 PSC meeting in Zagreb.
Progress has also been made on Act. 1.5 and 1.6, which focus on drafting plans and strategies at the regional and destination levels and identifying enabling conditions for their success. Act. 1.9 (coordinated by IUCN), related to capacity development for pilot regions and destinations and experience exchange, has delivered its first output, i.e. the Capacity Development Plan (Deliv. 1.9.1). This plan outlines training approaches and events throughout the project and includes several activities aimed at facilitating the exchange of best practices and experiences.
In Act. 1.11, the partnership has consistently published project-related news on the web, Facebook, and LinkedIn. Additionally, LP and HSPN launched in June 2024 two social media accounts for the project (LinkedIn and YouTube), and the project website was finalized and published on June 18, 2024. The partnership has attended several events, including the Community4Tourism Kick-Off Meeting and EuroMed training events. Internal communication remains active through regular emails and monthly online meetings. The project's Communication Strategy (part of Act. 1.11), developed by HSPN, has been also approved by the partnership.

In WP2, the Coordination Plan (Activity 2.1) was developed by CPMR and subsequently approved by the partnership. In Act. 2.2 all the five Regional Coordination Units (RCUs) were established. Each region has provided documents outlining the context, tasks, responsibilities, and functioning of their respective RCUs. In Act. 2.3 the selection of pilot destinations and "Pilot Site Coordinators" is nearly complete, with all pilot destinations chosen except for France, where alternatives are still being considered. Project Regions are finalizing the geographical scope of these destinations and appointing coordinators, under the supervision of the RCUs.
in parallel each RCU has started to work for the identification of main CC-related issues in coastal tourism (Act. 2.4) in order to start the draft of an integrated “Regional Tourism Climate Strategy” for mitigating and adapting the whole Regional coastline to the Climate Change.

IUCN (WP coordinator), developed the WP3 coordination plan. The first task involved defining for the PP Regions a common set of criteria for selecting and defining pilot destinations based on the IUCN Nature-based Solutions standard. This set of criteria was approved by the partnership. IUCN and CPMR were overseeing the selection of pilot destinations to ensure alignment with project goals. The appointed Pilot Site Coordinators have started establishing the Tourism Climate Action Task Force (Activity 3.2), which aims to engage key public and private stakeholders at the local level. Additionally, they have begun analyzing climate change-related issues, that will allow them to draft their Destination Tourism Climate Action Plan (Activity 3.4), and design/kick off at least one Nature-based Solution.
Through these ongoing efforts, the Natour4CChange project is making significant progress across all WPs, setting the stage for impactful climate resilience actions in Mediterranean tourism regions and destinations.

The NaTour4CChange project has made significant progress in advancing climate action within coastal tourism. Through active collaboration and coordination among partners, the project has successfully implemented key activities, delivering impactful outputs and fostering regional cooperation.
Natour4CChange partnership is meeting regularly online each month. These meetings are organized, coordinated, and reported by the LP. In the 2nd semester 5 PSC meetings took place online and one was held in-person (Almeria  - Spain, on 22.11.2024).
On 28th of November IFT has organized and moderated an online meeting with 14 Associate partners, to get them informed about first project results developed so far
As far as the WP1 “Methods and Learnings is concerned, Carbon footprint monitoring (Act. 1.2) is ongoing, with key data input by each PP in the Programme CO2.
Deliv. 1.3.1 “Report on best practices” has been issued in the 1st semester, as well as Deliv. 1.4.1 “methodological frameworks for assessing climate change (CC) mitigation and adaptation for the Regions” and Deliv. 1.4.2 “Methodological framework for assessing the main coastal tourism-related issues concerning CC, for the pilot Destinations”.
In this 2nd semester it was decided to merge Deliverables D.1.5.1, D.1.5.2, and D.1.6.1 into one document for improving their friendliness and usability.
Deliv. 1.5.1 “Technical report describing the methods and brief on how to apply them to develop the Regional Tourism Climate Strategy/Plan”, Deliv 1.5.2 “Technical report describing the methods and brief on how to apply them, to develop the Destination Tourism Climate Strategy/Plan”, and Deliv 1.6.1 “Technical Report describing the identified enabling conditions, with a checklist for supporting regions” were issued, thanks to the contribution of all the PPs. A specific training session on these methods and tools was held in person in Almeria (Spain) on 21.11.2024, and repeated online on January 15th 2025.
As far as the WP2 “Regional Tourism Climate Governance & Policy” is concerned, "Establishment of the Regional Coordination Unit for Climate Action in Tourism” (Act. 2.2) was implemented in all the 5 Regions (although its formalization was delayed in Sardinia and Crete – see below).
Pilot destination selection and "Pilot site Coordinator" appointment (Act. 2.3) were accomplished in 5 out of 6 countries (in France the process took longer time – see below).
D.2.4.1 “Reports compiling the assessment results in project regions” were finalized by 4 out of 5 Regions (Crete had some troubles – see below).
Concerning the “Coordination with the missions governance projects (TCP & IDP) and JS” (Act. 2.8) project’s representatives attended the Dialogue4Tourism conference in Poreč – Croatia on 10 April 2024 and the MED Cluster Climate Change & Biodiversity meeting in Rome on 11.4.2024. Finally, about WP3 “Pilot Destination Resilience”, D.3.1.1 “Criteria for pilot destinations selection” were developed in the 1st semester, while the establishment of a Tourism Climate Action Taskforce (Act. 3.2) took place in 4 out of 6 pilot areas (Crete and French pilotes having some delays – see below).
D.3.3.1 “Reports compiling the climate assessment results of each pilot Destination” were finalized by 3 out of 6 pilot areas (Spanish, Crete and French pilotes having some delays – see below).
The drafting of the “Destination Tourism Climate Action Strategy/Plan” (Act. 3.4) started in all the 6 pilot areas with the training held in Almeria in November, supported also by a individual support from WP Coordinator and Plan Bleu.
As additional contribution (not planned in the project AF), Plan Bleu - with internal resources, using its innovative, open-access tool, MapX - developed interactive online maps on vulnerability degree of project pilot Regions and destinations, based on the analysis carried out by each concerned PP. This represents an added value for Plan Bleu (that is the "Mediterranean Observatory on Environment and Sustainable Development of the Barcelona Convention") but also for the project, that gains a worldwide known "window" on its outputs.
During the third reporting period, the NaTour4CChange (NT4CC) partnership strengthened coordination and implementation across all Work Packages, ensuring effective monitoring, technical cooperation, and dissemination of results. Governance was maintained through regular monthly online meetings of the Project Steering Committee (PSC) and Technical Committees. Five PSC meetings were held online and moderated by the Lead Partner, complemented by an in-person meeting in Marseille on 9–10 April 2025. This event was a milestone, combining a formal PSC session with training on Nature-based Solutions (NbS, Act. 1.9), an exchange workshop among pilot destinations, and a study visit, reinforcing learning, peer exchange, and project visibility.
Under WP1, activities advanced steadily. Carbon footprint monitoring (Act. 1.2), coordinated by HSPN, was consolidated: partners submitted data on schedule, while reporting for Periods 1 and 2 was finalised and analysed, producing trends and recommendations. Awareness-raising visits to pilot sites were also carried out. NbS work (Act. 1.7) culminated in the publication of Deliverable 1.7.1, NbS for Coastal Destinations Climate Action Manual, prepared by IUCN with Plan Bleu, followed by in-person training in Marseille to strengthen partner capacity. IFT advanced Activity 1.8 on destination marketing and visitor communication, defining methodology, draft structure, and partner consultations. Dissemination and communication (Act. 1.11) were actively pursued: IFT, CPMR, IUCN, and Plan Bleu promoted results at events such as the Dialogue4Tourism session (Brussels, May 2024), the UN Ocean Conference (Nice, 2025), and the EUSAIR Conference (April 2025). Natura Jadera updated its website and social media, uploaded the project video, and prepared content for the official webpage. HSPN produced new communication tools (video, roll-up banner), managed the website and LinkedIn updates, coordinated event logistics, and drafted the first NT4CC Newsletter.
In WP2, major progress was made on Regional Tourism Climate Strategies. Five regional assessments (Act. 2.4) were delivered, providing a solid foundation for strategic planning. Based on these, three regions finalised their strategies (Zadar County, Andalusia, and Crete) under Activity 2.5. All partners contributed by preparing inputs on enabling conditions and working through their Regional Coordination Units. Capacity-building was reinforced with a second online training on the Climate Strategy Toolkit (January 2025), complemented by bilateral follow-ups. Cross-learning was promoted through the 1st Transnational Exchange Workshop for Regional Cross-Fertilisation (18 June 2025), organised by CPMR with over 40 participants, including associated partners such as the Balearic Islands. The workshop enabled presentation of strategies, exchange of practices, and peer review by The Travel Foundation.
WP3 saw tangible progress in Destination Climate Action Plans and NbS pilots. Six technical assessment reports (Act. 3.3) were delivered, covering Cabo de Gata (Spain), Crete (Greece), Dugi Otok (Croatia), Hutovo Blato (Bosnia and Herzegovina), Villasimius (Italy), and Cap Ferrat (France). These identified climate vulnerabilities and tourism pressures. Work then advanced on Destination Climate Action Plans (Act. 3.4): Deliverable 3.4.1 was submitted by AndTour (Andalusia) and Natura Jadera (Dugi Otok), while four other plans are underway and scheduled for Period 4. NbS design and testing (Act. 3.5) was launched, guided by the NbS Manual and Marseille training. Preparations for interventions began, including Natura Jadera’s wooden walkway at Sakarun beach to address erosion at a high-pressure site. Local workshops, such as Beaulieu-sur-Mer, validated assessments and engaged stakeholders. The first Destination Exchange Workshop (Act. 3.7), held in Marseille, allowed pilot destinations to share methods, identify challenges, and build synergies.
Dissemination remained strong and cross-cutting. CPMR promoted NT4CC through its commissions and political meetings, while IUCN and Plan Bleu ensured visibility in global fora. Natura Jadera and AndTour supported outreach via institutional channels and media coverage. The Region of Crete promoted the project locally through World Wetlands Day at Alatsolimni, co-organised with HSPN and local authorities, raising awareness of pilot sites and project objectives.
Overall, the third reporting period consolidated NT4CC’s analytical and strategic foundations. Regional and destination assessments were largely completed, three regional strategies finalised, and preparatory steps for NbS interventions launched. Capacity-building and exchange activities reinforced the Mediterranean community of practice on climate-resilient coastal tourism. These results have set the stage for the next period, when remaining strategies will be completed and NbS pilots more fully implemented, ensuring that NT4CC delivers on its objectives.

fr.name: La gouvernance du tourisme durable dans des territoires à haute valeur environnementale : le lien entre tourisme et nature pour faire face à la crise climatique avec une approche écosystémique
fr.description:
La Méditerranée est une zone vulnérable dans l’état d'urgence climatique actuel. Le tourisme est l'un des secteurs économiques les plus touchés, avec des risques accrus, notamment les inondations et l’érosion côtière. Le parcours de transition pour le tourisme et la Déclaration de Glasgow ont créé une dynamique mondiale pour l’action climatique dans le tourisme, mais les décideurs politiques et les destinations ont besoin de soutien et de nouvelles approches. La nature peut être une pierre angulaire pour relever ce défi, tout en assurant la protection des atouts essentiels, tels que les plages. Comment? Grâce à l'atténuation et l'adaptation au changement climatique avec des approches écosystémiques, qui ne peuvent être accomplies que grâce à une gouvernance forte, une planification solide et l'engagement du secteur privé et des citoyens.
NaTour4CChange s’appuiera sur des expériences réussies pour tester des solutions visant à accroître la résilience des destinations côtières en Méditerranée. Le projet établira des méthodes communes pour permettre aux régions participantes d'évaluer leurs priorités d'adaptation et d'atténuation liées au tourisme, et d'agir pour le climat à travers des plans et des stratégies, en s'appuyant sur la collaboration. Des équipes intersectorielles fourniront des plans d'action climatique spécifiques au tourisme, en se concentrant sur l’adaptation, où des Solutions fondées sur la Nature seront testées pour garantir leur faisabilité, tandis que des approches novatrices en matière de marketing et de communication engageront les acteurs privés, les visiteurs et les résidents. NaTour4CChange encouragera la fertilisation croisée entre les régions et les destinations pour parvenir à des méthodes communes, ce qui conduira à des leçons, des meilleures pratiques et des recommandations. Les partenaires associés et les parties prenantes externes bénéficieront de ces apprentissages afin d'apporter une contribution de la Méditerranée à ce défi mondial.

fr.name: Tester le paradigme de Communautés Energétiques et la résilience climatique dans les zones situées derrière les Med ports vers la neutralité des émissions et la réduction de la pauvreté énergétique
fr.description: EnerCmed propose un nouveau paradigme pour l’arrière-pays énergétique positif et résilient au climat qui est centré sur le concept de communautés d’énergie renouvelable (CER) pour permettre la régénération énergétique des quartiers marginalisés de l’arrière-pays portuaire, normalement habités par des personnes exposées à des risques de pauvreté énergétique.Le paradigme est testé par l’activation de 6 CER dans l’arrière-pays marginalisé de Gênes (1), Valence (1), Patras (2), Pula (1) et Novigrad (1) pour rassembler 345 familles / utilisateurs exposés à la précarité énergétique, générer 278 MWh / an à partir de sources renouvelables (-160 tonnes de CO2 / an) et réduire de 15 à 20% les factures d’énergie.Pour chaque CER , un investissement dans la solution basée sur la nature (NBS) est effectué dans les quartiers cibles en tant que dissipateur thermique naturel pour équilibrer la consommation d’énergie de refroidissement résidentiel et contracter les phénomènes d’îlot de chaleur urbain.L’idée innovante est d’interpoler –de la conceptualisation du paradigme à la démonstration– 3 principes techniques tels que #CER #urban phénomènes d’îlots de chaleur # protection contre la pauvreté. Dans cette perspective,EnerCmed intègre le principe de planification énergétique au niveau de la dimension climatique et sociale des quartiers marginalisés.Dans EnerCmed, la pierre angulaire de la coopération transnationale est représentée par l’instrument de facilité de connaissances (KFI), un mécanisme de soutien complet dans le cadre duquel un pool de partenaires hautement spécialisés aide le partenaire pilote à transposer le plan d’action dans des projets pilotes applicatifs de CER-NBS.EnerCmed répond à l’objectif d’INTERREG MED de faciliter les plans de transition énergétique/résilience dans les villes,renforcer l’engagement des citoyens pour un espace de vie plus durable et fournit un programme de réplication multi-villes pour permettre à d’autres villes MED d’adopter les solutions

fr.actual_achievements:
Au cours de sa première année, EnerCmed a réalisé des avancées significatives en posant les bases communes pour le déploiement des Communautés d’Énergie Renouvelable (REC) associées à des Solutions Basées sur la Nature (NBS) dans cinq villes portuaires méditerranéennes. Le projet s’est concentré sur l’identification des conditions techniques, réglementaires, environnementales et sociales influençant la faisabilité des modèles REC-NBS dans les quartiers urbains vulnérables. À travers des évaluations conjointes, l’engagement des acteurs locaux et la co-conception d’outils stratégiques, les partenaires ont préparé le terrain pour une mise en œuvre concrète visant à lutter contre la précarité énergétique et à renforcer la résilience climatique dans les quartiers marginalisés.

Deux études clés ont soutenu ce travail : l’Analyse de Contexte (D1.1.1), qui a cartographié les conditions juridiques, infrastructurelles et sociales ; et la Cartographie des REC activables (D1.2.1), qui a identifié les sites propices à l’implantation des REC et a favorisé l’engagement local à travers des Journées Portes Ouvertes dans toutes les villes pilotes.

Les progrès se sont poursuivis avec la rédaction du Plan d’Action pour les Communautés d’Énergie Renouvelable de l’Hinterland, qui guidera la mise en œuvre des pilotes, ainsi que la définition d’un Cahier des Charges commun pour la création des REC. Parallèlement, le projet a élaboré le Portefeuille de Solutions Basées sur la Nature à Petite Échelle (D2.4.1), offrant aux villes pilotes un catalogue d’interventions vertes reproductibles — telles que des toitures végétalisées, des surfaces perméables et des structures d’ombrage — afin d’atténuer les effets des vagues de chaleur et de réduire la demande en climatisation.

L’Instrument de Soutien Technique (KFI) a été lancé pour fournir un appui technique et des outils partagés à l’ensemble du partenariat. Le projet a également initié le suivi de son propre bilan carbone via la plateforme ZeroCO2.

La présence numérique d’EnerCmed a été établie à travers un site web, une page LinkedIn et une vidéo institutionnelle, favorisant la communication à la fois avec les communautés locales et les parties prenantes du secteur. Cette première année a mis en évidence la valeur de la coopération transnationale — combinant des méthodologies communes avec des adaptations locales — pour concevoir des solutions inclusives et reproductibles.

en.name: Testing energy-community & climate-resilient integrated paradigm for carbon neutrality and energy poverty shielding in MED city-port hinterlands
en.description:
EnerCmed proposes of a new paradigm for energy-positive & climate resilient hinterlands that is centred on the concept of Renewable Energy Communities (REC) and that has the ambition to enable the energy regeneration of marginalized neighborhood in port hinterlands, normally inhabited by people exposed to risks of energy poverty. The paradigm is tested by the activation of 7 RECs in marginalized hinterlands of Genova (1), Valencia (2), Patras (2), Pula (1) & Novigrad (1) to gather 345 families/users exposed to energy poverty, generate 278 MWh/y from renewable sources corresponding to 160 tons CO2/year reduction and reduce by 15-20% the energy bills. For each REC created one Nature Based Solution (NBS) investment is carried on in the target neighborhoods as natural heat sink to balance the residential cooling energy-use & contract the Urban Heat Island phenomena.
The innovative idea is to interpolate – from paradigm conceptualization to test demonstration - 3 technical-driven principles such as #energy communities #urban heat islands phenomena and #energy poverty shielding. In this perspective, EnerCmed integrates traditional REC trilemma (engaging people, ideal business model and governance structure, system engineering/interoperability/digitalization) with energy planning principle at micro-site climate & social dimension (marginalized neighborhoods) level. In EnerCmed, the cornerstone of the transnational cooperation is represented by the Knowledge Facility Instrument (KFI), a comprehensive support-mechanisms where a pool of highly specialized partners helps the pilot partner to transpose the transnational Hinterland Renewables Communities Action Plan in applicative REC-NBS pilots. EnerCmed responds to INTERREG MED goal of facilitate plans&strategies for energy transition and resilience in cities and reinforce citizens engagement for more sustainable living area and delivers a multi-city replication programme to enable further MED cities to adopt the solutions
en.expected_achievements:
To design a common & innovative MED-perspective paradigm of energy-positive & climate resilient hinterland around the concept of Renewable Energy Communities coupled with Nature Based Solutions to contrast energy poverty and offset REC externalities
Transposing the Hinterland RC Action Plan into the deployment of 7 pilot RECs in Genova (1), Valencia (2), Patras (2), Pula (1) & Novigrad (1) to be further coupled with 7 Nature based interventions to balance the residential cooling energy-use  
Deliver specialized MED multi-cities transnational replication programme and strategies to support the large-scale uptake of coupled REC & NBS paradigm in Mediterranean area, as option for an affordable e-transition of marginalized neighborhoods.
en.actual_achievements:
During the first reporting period, significant progress was made in various project activities, primarily within Work Package 1 (WP1): Transnational Paradigm of Energy-Positive & Climate-Resilient Mediterranean Hinterlands Centered on Renewable Energy Communities (REC) & Nature-Based Solutions (NBS). The work packages were subdivided into activities led by different partners, fostering a truly collaborative approach. The key achievements include:
-Strategic Assessment for REC Development: A comprehensive Background Analysis for pilot development was carried out. This included legal and normative dashboards, spatial planning, critical infrastructure audits, micro-climate analysis of Urban Heat Islands (UHI) at neighborhood levels, and social composition and energy poverty reviews. These analyses were tailored for each of the partner cities, with contributions from UNIGE, SIN, UCY, VCE, IRENA, PAT, MGE, ETRA, and LAR.
-Mapping of REC Potential: REC activation potential was mapped for pilot projects. Local stakeholder groups were engaged to initiate a co-design process. Open Days were organized in the five pilot cities to explain the benefits and potential of RECs, aiming to garner citizen support. While some Open Days have already been held, others are planned.
-Establishment of the Transnational Knowledge Facility Instrument (KFI): This tool has been successfully created to mentor pilot city investments during the test phase (WP2) and facilitate knowledge transfer during the replication phase (WP3). The KFI meets regularly, and partners such as UNIGE, SIN, UCY, PAT, and ETRA are actively involved.
-Carbon Footprint Monitoring: All project partners have put in place mechanisms to regulate and monitor the project’s carbon footprint in alignment with the overall environmental goals.
-NBS portfolio: Draft results for a portfolio of small-scale nature-based solutions (NBS) have been developed, led by UCY.
Key meetings and events included a kick-off meeting and technical workshop in Genoa (organized by UNIGE), monthly KFI meetings, Open Days with local communities and stakeholders (e.g., in Genoa), and participation in national conferences:
2.Project performance monitoring 
To ensure smooth project implementation, internal deadlines were established for submitting drafts and preliminary results of activities. So far, a few delays have occurred but have not affected the overall delivery timeline of key activities. No significant issues with performance or spending deviations have been encountered.
3.	Progress 
During this reporting period, significant progress was made across multiple work packages, with each partner playing a vital role in advancing their respective tasks. The collaborative efforts and specialized contributions ensured a well-coordinated approach, fostering the achievement of key milestones. Specific examples of partner involvement include:
WP1 Transnational paradigm of energy-positive & climate resilient MED hinterlands centred on REC & NBS:
-	A 1.1. UNIGE took a leadership role by organizing the kick-off meeting and technical workshop in Genoa, with participation from all project partners. This provided a platform for aligning strategies and work plans.

-	A 1.1. Strategic Assessments and Background Analysis: UNIGE led the strategic assessments, while background analysis activities were carried out by project partners according to their respective pilot cities: MSE for Genoa, VCE for Valencia, IRENA for Pula and Novigrad, and PAT for Patras. This collaboration ensured tailored analyses that reflect each city's unique characteristics and needs.

-	A 1.2 Open Days and Local Stakeholder Engagement: The organization of open days and the activation of local stakeholders for the co-design process were critical to the project’s success. This activity, led by MSE, saw contributions from UNIGE, MSE for Genoa, VCE for Valencia, IRENA for Pula and Novigrad, and PAT for Patras, emphasizing the widespread engagement of local communities.

-	A 1.4 Transnational Knowledge Facility Instrument (KFI): UNIGE, SIN, UCY, PAT, and ETRA actively participated in the formation and ongoing operations of the KFI. This activity, led by SIN, ensures the transnational exchange of knowledge and experience, reinforcing cooperation among project partners.

-	A 1.5 Carbon Footprint Monitoring: Partners including UNIGE, SIN, UCY, VCE, IRENA, PAT, MGE, ETRA, and LAR were deeply involved in the carbon footprint monitoring processes. This activity, jointly led by SIN and UNIGE, is a critical aspect of the project, ensuring that the environmental impacts are continuously monitored and regulated.

WP2 Testing applicative models for emergence energy & climate positive neighborhoods combining REC & NBS
-	A 2.4 Nature-Based Solutions Portfolio: UCY took the lead in developing the portfolio of small-scale nature-based solutions, contributing to the overall goal of enhancing climate resilience in the Mediterranean hinterlands.

In its first year, EnerCmed made solid progress in building a shared foundation for deploying Renewable Energy Communities (RECs) coupled with Nature-Based Solutions (NBS) in five Mediterranean port cities. The project focused on identifying the technical, regulatory, environmental, and social conditions that influence the feasibility of REC-NBS models in vulnerable urban areas. Through joint assessments, local engagement, and the co-design of strategic tools, partners prepared the ground for real-world implementation aimed at tackling energy poverty and enhancing climate resilience in marginalized districts.

Two key studies supported this work: the Background Analysis (D1.1.1), which mapped legal, infrastructural, and social conditions; and the Mapping of Activable RECs (D1.2.1), which identified viable locations for REC deployment and supported local engagement through Open Days in all pilot cities.

Progress continued with the drafting of the Hinterland Renewables Communities Action Plan, which will guide pilot implementation, and the definition of a common Terms of Reference for REC setup. In parallel, the project delivered the Portfolio of Small-Scale Nature-Based Solutions (D2.4.1), offering pilot cities a catalogue of replicable green interventions—such as green roofs, permeable surfaces, and shading structures—to help mitigate heat stress and reduce cooling demand.

The Knowledge Facility Instrument (KFI) was launched to provide technical support and shared tools across the partnership. The project also started monitoring its own carbon footprint through the ZeroCO2 platform.

EnerCmed’s digital presence was established through a website, LinkedIn page, and institutional video, supporting communication with both local communities and sector stakeholders. The year highlighted the value of transnational cooperation—combining shared methodologies with local adaptation to shape inclusive, scalable solutions.
During its first 18 months, EnerCmed has completed the conceptual phase and launched the implementation of pilot actions, while also setting the basis for replication in new Mediterranean cities. The project has already delivered concrete results and demonstrated the added value of working transnationally.

Conceptual framework (WP1):
The consortium finalised the Terms of Reference (D1.3.1), a common methodological framework for Renewable Energy Communities (RECs) in marginalised port districts. The document integrates legal, financial, technical, and social aspects, and was refined step by step with partner contributions.
The Knowledge Facility Instrument (KFI) was established with its guidelines and first training materials (videos, infographics, technical checklists). It now acts as a mentoring and knowledge-sharing tool for pilot cities and follower municipalities.
Partners contributed to the carbon footprint monitoring, ensuring the project aligns with Euro-MED environmental standards.

Pilot preparation and activation (WP2):
All pilot cities (Genoa, Valencia, Patras, Pula, Novigrad, and Larnaka) advanced in preparing or activating RECs and Nature-Based Solutions (NBS).
In Genoa, one photovoltaic (PV) plant is already operational at Volta school, while a second is under development at Ferrero school, combined with an educational NBS garden.
In Valencia, two schools were chosen: Ballester Fandos (PV + NBS) and Ausiàs March (community engagement and NBS). Families, teachers, and local NGOs are directly involved.
In Patras, feasibility assessments and stakeholder engagement were completed to support pilot REC activation.
In Pula and Novigrad, technical designs for PV systems and NBS measures were finalised, with local approvals secured.
In Larnaka, the municipality prepared its role as lighthouse meta-replicator and successfully hosted the 3rd Steering Committee and a scientific symposium.
Deliverable D2.2.1 Preliminary assessments consolidated technical and economic sustainability analyses across all pilots.
UCY produced the first Portfolio of small-scale NBS (D2.4.1), offering practical design solutions adapted to Mediterranean microclimates.
ETRA advanced the Energy Tracking Platform, defining key indicators, data flows, and digital functionalities for common monitoring.

Replication (WP3):
The MED Multi-City Replication Programme was launched, including a call for new follower cities and the preparation of an info webinar.
Work has started on the MED Joint Strategy and the Atlas of coupled REC/NBS, which will transfer project knowledge to a wider network of Mediterranean municipalities.

Communication and engagement:
Over a dozen Open Days, workshops, and conferences were organised in partner countries, reaching local citizens, schools, NGOs, and public authorities.
EnerCmed launched its newsletter, while partners produced infographics, videos, and media articles to improve visibility and awareness.
The Larnaka Symposium (May 2025) brought together academics, municipalities, and NGOs, reinforcing the scientific and practical dimension of EnerCmed.

fr.name: PRÉVENIR ET RÉDUIRE LA MONTAGNE DE DÉCHETS TEXTILES DANS LA ZONE MED
fr.description:
Dans la période actuelle où les challenges et les opportunités liées aux textiles usagés sont de plus en plus dans la ligne de mire des gouvernements, de l’industrie et de la société civile, des changements importants doivent être faits pour une transition vers une économie circulaire pour les textiles et les vêtements (T/V). Les exécutifs locaux, régionaux et nationaux ont un rôle primordial à jouer afin de créer les bonnes conditions et inciter à accélérer cette transition.
Pour clore la boucle textile, tous les acteurs de la chaine de valeur T/V doivent trouver ensemble le bon modèle économique.
Dans leur processus, les institutions doivent réussir à inclure des organisations qui n’ont pas encore trouvé la solution ; celles qui sont chargés de la gestion de la fin de vie des textiles, mais aussi celles qui collectent, trient, recyclent, et celles qui s’orientent vers la ré-utilisation et les process de création de nouveaux produits.
VERDEinMED a pour objet de proposer un cadre de travail pour étudier des réussites de business modèles circulaires, en identifiant les besoins d’innovation dans les modèles économiques, en matière technologique et sociale, ainsi que dans les contextes législatifs et d’usage.
En commençant par la refondation de l’industrie reposant sur des pratiques durables, et orientant la demande vers une consommation consciente, ce projet aboutira à la définition du cadre nécessaire à la transition du secteur des T/V vers la durabilité et la circularité.
L’écosystème ainsi créé sera soutenu au travers de la Bussola Verde, une « boussole verte » qui intégrera la création de hubs régionaux, de plateformes de savoirs et de connaissances, et un service support en une seule entité, regroupant des experts et des expertises venues de différents horizons, différents territoires, afin de préparer la constitution de centres de connaissance et de compétences, sortes de lieux uniques de convergence, dans chaque région.
fr.expected_achievements:
Explore and examine economic paradigms and new forms of value creation and implement circularity principles to reshape existing business models
Support and accelerate sustainable practices by involving industry, consumers and public authorities in demand driven and evidence based testing activities
Support public authorities in the development and adoption of strategies leading to the transition to a circular T/C economy on local/regional level

en.name: PreVEnting and ReDucing the tExtiles waste mountain in the MED area
en.description:
While the challenges and opportunities of used textiles are increasingly in the spotlight of governments, industry, and civil society, considerable system changes are required to transition towards a circular economy for textiles and clothing (T/C). Local, regional, national governments can and should play a pivotal role to create the right conditions and incentives to accelerate this transition.
In order to close the textiles loop all players within the T/C value chain need to be able to create a viable business case. In their engagement processes, governments will need to include those organisations that have not yet been part of the solution: those in charge of the end-of-use textiles value chain such as waste management companies as collectors, sorters, recyclers, but also end users who will be involved in the co creation process of new products.
VERDEinMED will present an analytical framework to study the successful implementation of circular business models, identifying the needs in terms of business model innovation, technological innovation and social innovation, and the enabling conditions of policy and behavioural change.
VERDE project will show the full pallet of a threefold support service addressed to civil society, industry and policy makers indicating tailor made interventions needed for this transition. 
Starting with the reformation the industry to use sustainable practices, orienting the demand towards conscious consumption it will conclude by defining a policy framework to support the transition of the T/C sector towards sustainability and circularity.
The ecosystem thus created, will be sustained through the Verde Bussola, a Green Compass integrating the creation of regional hubs, knowledge platform and support service into a single entity, bringing together experts and knowledge from different locations in order to prepare the framework for the constitution of knowledge and competence centers as a one stop shop in each region.
en.expected_achievements:
Explore and examine economic paradigms and new forms of value creation and implement circularity principles to reshape existing business models
Support and accelerate sustainable practices by involving industry, consumers and public authorities in demand driven and evidence based testing activities
Support public authorities in the development and adoption of strategies leading to the transition to a circular T/C economy on local/regional level
en.actual_achievements:
During the initial reporting period, PPs successfully executed all planned activities without exception. LP1 CERTH, organized the first in-person kick-off meeting involving both Project Partners and Associated Partners. This meeting took place in Kastoria, GR on the 6th and 7th of February 2024. The primary purpose of this kick-off meeting was to coordinate and monitor the tasks and activities outlined in the work packages, ensuring the timely and quality delivery of results and deliverables.
Throughout the first six months of the project, several online meetings were held among the project partners to monitor and coordinate the implementation of the work packages. All PPs played an active role in Work Package 1, particularly in the development of the deliverable titled “D.1.2.2. Regional Reports of State of the Art of Textile Waste Management and Recycling.” PPs contributed significantly by providing a comprehensive overview of the European approach to textile waste management and recycling. This included addressing various issues, relevant legislation, and socio-behavioral aspects across Europe. Additionally, PPs gathered feedback specific to their national/regional context, detailing the regional and national framework for waste management and recycling. The deliverable has been finalized and uploaded to the basecamp. 
PPs contributed to deliverable D.1.1.1, known as the Project Management and Financial Handbook, which has been finalized and uploaded to the basecamp. This handbook serves as a crucial support tool, outlining the fundamental guidelines related to the VERDEinMED Programme procedures. Within the reporting period, two out of seven webinars were organized, namely: “Global Dimension of Textiles” and “Ecodesign Strategies”. 
In relation to Work Package 2, PPs contributed to the development of the deliverable D.2.5.1, the Communication Strategy, which is finalized and uploaded on the basecamp. As part of this effort, PPs created and disseminated several posts on social media networks to enhance the project's visibility and outreach.
For Work Package 3, within Act. 3.4, Staff Members from CERTH and CTA as communication officer participated in person in the 2nd MED Innovation Camp which took place in Barcelona, from May 27th - 30th. In this Innovation Camp they represented the VERDEinMED Project and participated in the co-creation workshops on the Smart specialisation strategies supporting Mediterranean green and just transition.
On the 17th of June, CERTH, CTA and TTA-TC participated in the Innovation Days in Marseille. Key challenges identified based on a co-creational approach: Regulatory Alignment -Cross-Border Collaboration - Sustainable Funding. 
For Act. 3.5, LEG prepared the necessary Excel file to calculate the carbon footprint associated with travel, accommodation, and event organization. This was accomplished using the tool provided by the Euro MED programme, ensuring accurate and comprehensive environmental impact assessments. All PPs provided the required information by filling out the provided file. 
In summary, during the initial reporting period, all PPs fulfilled their responsibilities across multiple work packages, contributing to critical deliverables and ensuring effective project management, coordination, and communication. The activities undertaken by WP and Activity Leaders were essential in laying a strong foundation for the VERDEinMED Project, ensuring its alignment with planned objectives and fostering collaboration among partners.

The VERDEinMED project has made significant progress in its mission to promote sustainability within the textile and clothing sector. Several important activities have been completed, contributing to the transition towards more sustainable practices in the industry. One of the key milestones in the project was the finalization of the Project Management and Financial Handbook by CERTH, which provided essential guidance on the project's financial and operational management. Along with this, the development of the HUB Stakeholder Engagement Guidance document helped to create a stronger connection between the various partners and stakeholders involved in the project. All partners were engaged in the creation of the VERDEinMED Hubs and participated in numerous online meetings, ensuring active communication and collaboration. Training and upskilling were another key aspect of the project. Activity 1.3, led by CITEVE, involved the design and delivery of a comprehensive training program aimed at improving the skills of PPs and APs in the textile sector. This program, which concluded in December 2024, featured seven workshops that covered essential topics such as ecodesign strategies, circular business models, and promoting sustainable consumption practices. A total of 187 participants were trained, contributing to the final deliverables including training materials, Knowledge Platform content, communication campaigns, and living labs. These deliverables will help ensure that the project's objectives, which include fostering sustainability in the textile industry, are met and maintained in the long term.
A crucial part of the project’s success is the establishment of the Support Service, a tailored technical assistance service for a diverse range of stakeholders, including SMEs, startups, research organizations, consumers, and policymakers. In addition to these efforts, Activity 2.3 saw the creation of a guidance document by LEG, which outlined strategies for preparing communication campaigns and organizing living labs. In Activity 3.4, CERTH, DIADYMA, TTA-TC and CTA participated in the1st and 2nd ISE Community of Practice events which took place on 18-19 of June and 14th of November respectively. CTA also participated in online events such as Innovative Sustainable Economy Community (ISEC) Hub Launch Event that took place on Wednesday 2 October 2024  online; as well as in the dissemination of the ISE mission content in the project social media.  In Activity 3.5, all project partners contributed to the calculation of the project’s carbon footprint, with LEG proposing strategies to reduce and improve the project’s environmental impact.
The added value of the cooperation between all partners in the VERDEinMED project is evident. Each partner brought their expertise to different aspects of the project, ensuring a well-rounded approach. Through the development of the Knowledge Platform and the implementation of living labs, the project is promoting knowledge-sharing across the textile sector, helping to drive long-term sustainability. Furthermore, the project is not only focusing on the practical aspects of textiles' sustainability but also on improving the project's overall environmental footprint, as evidenced by the carbon footprint calculations and proposed improvements.

en.name: Investing in Green and Just Transition for a New Sustainable Business as Usual Economy
en.description:
Urban and urban-rural areas in the MED region are under threat due to negative impacts of climate change and unsustainable development practices. This calls for a change from an old to a new economy with new consumer, business and financing models that will lead towards resource efficient New Business as Usual (BaU) Economy, making the Green and Just Transition (GJT) a functional reality.
BauNOW project main objective is to improve the planning and implementing capacities of public and private stakeholders for financing of measures supporting the GJT to climate-neutral and resilient living areas. With new, integrated development practices and improved access to finances for GJT, both the public and private sector in the MED project areas will recognise the business and development potential of GJT, making it their new BaU.
The GJT Dashboard is the project’s solution that will facilitate this process. An easy-to-use web-based tool with step-by-step methodology on how to design, finance and implement GJT on local or regional level will serve public authorities, practitioners and private sector. It will be validated through bottom-up processes in 9 pilot areas in 7 MED countries, leading to concrete measures in support of GJT. Transferability plan, focused on access to public and private investments for GJT, will transfer the Dashboard to other MED regions and beyond through Community of practitioners, investment forums and partnership networks.
Project regions and partners represent the diversity of the MED area and aim to bridge its regional disparities by joint development of effective, easily transferable solutions for GJT. 
BauNOW is a new outlook on development. It delivers the Dashboard tool to support the implementation of a paradigm shift in development planning, going beyond sectoral approaches, and calling for and opening opportunities for public and private sector to re-evaluate investment principles and recognise the potential of GJT as viable investment.
en.expected_achievements:
To raise capacities and strengthen cooperation of pilot area stakeholders for planning and financing the Green and Just Transition process by integrating and adapting best available approaches, tools and know-how into a GJT demonstration methodology.
To develop the Green and Just Transition Dashboard, a comprehensive tool supporting integrated planning and financing of GJT, by testing and validating the draft demonstration methodology in bottom-up participatory processes in 9 project areas.
To improve access to GJT funding and ensure long-term sustainability, mainstreaming and transferability of the developed Dashboard through established international Community of Practitioners and Investors in the MED area and beyond.
en.actual_achievements:
In this reporting period all the tasks were successfully conducted. The project started with the kick-off on February 16th. The purpose of the meeting was the presentation of lead partner (LP) Geodetic Institute of Slovenia (GIS) to acquaint all partners with the partnership, provide a general outline of the project's context and content, share administrative information and provide information on the kick-off meeting in Ljubljana. Also the short overview of the project was presented by the LP about the objectives, activities, main outputs per WP, project overall objective, project work plan and project time plan.

The LP together with PPs organized In-Person two-day project meeting, which was scheduled in March 20th -21st in Ljubljana. Project partners participated in the meeting (a few of them were present online). The aim of the meeting was to introduce each other, to start the project, to unify the understanding of the project and start brainstorming about pilots. WP leaders presented activities in their WPs. LP presented key administrative and technical issues regarding project implementation including status of contracting activities, project reporting and management of the project. Project partners gave a short presentation of their work on the specific theme. They provided key information about their past and current activities, as well as potential ideas and initiatives for their future work regarding pilot activities, their experiences and knowledge .PPs presented the ideas and initiatives that are the drivers of individual “pilot” that will be developed and implemented through the BauNOW project. Project partners discussed the synergies between pilots, the type of cooperation that is needed and where they see the opportunities for connection. Some partners already made and presented drafts of implementation plans for their pilots.

LP attended Green Living Areas Thematic Community Kick-off event on10 – 11 April 2024 in Turin where LP was presented project BaunOW  and participated at GLA workshops.

On May 2024 LP organized online working meeting concerning the general concept of the project with core PPs and KOM leader. Before the meeting a chart was sent to partners outlining a proposal for a general spatial planning and investment process into sub-phases, providing descriptions for better comprehension and the general investment life cycle process for green projects (activity 1.1). The chart serves as a step-by-step guide, detailing key phases and stakeholders involved in the participatory process. 

Based on the meeting,  partners were asked to reflect on a concrete green project (such as climate change initiatives, nature-based solutions, mobility projects, energy initiatives, revitalization efforts, etc.) that they've either worked on or implemented in the past and evaluate its planning process. Partners provided feedback, some made an updated concept based on their project. The proposals have been taken into account in the upgrading of the concept. 

LP also participated at Green Living Areas  BauNOW 1st Green Energy Systems Thematic Working Group Meeting  11.6.2024 and contribute to discussion.
In September 2024, a joint working meeting was organised by LP to review project progress, outline the next steps, and to update the methodology for green investments. The agenda included presentations by REAN on the updated concept for the GJT Dashboard specifications, as well as discussions on administrative and reporting issues and future activities. At the end of September, further updates on the GJT Dashboard concept were presented. Following the presentation, the LP provided feedback to ensure the dashboard would be both feasible and aligned with the project's ambitions.
In October 2024, the LP introduced the Euro-MED Programme’s new carbon footprint monitoring initiative. To simplify the process, the LP provided an Excel sheet for data collection.

To better understand the progress of the pilot cases and offer support where needed, individual meetings were held with each partner, as well as with LP and partner Iskriva, in late September and early October. These meetings focused on discussing pilot-specific goals and planning the necessary steps to achieve them. All pilot cases were further developed by project partners, with meetings held with key stakeholders and territorial analyses conducted as needed. Some pilot cases were already launched, including an AI-powered public service solution offering a One-Stop-Shop service for citizens to receive tailored advice on energy renovation and renewable energy installations.
In November 2024, the partnership held a working meeting to present an upgraded concept for the GJT Dashboard. The dashboard was developed by REAN with support from the LP and Iskriva.

To facilitate the monitoring of target group engagement, the LP created a Google spreadsheet for partners to document the target groups engaged during the reporting period.

In early December 2024, the LP organized a joint event in Ravne na Koroškem, attended by Judith Cazas from the programme office, who delivered a presentation on the requirements for implementing investments. During the event, each partner provided a brief five-minute presentation on their pilot activities, and the GJT Dashboard was further discussed. The event also included the Conference on Investments in the Green Transition in Remote Industrial Regions on December 4, 2024. This conference was a collaborative initiative between the Baunow project and the NONA (New Governance for New Spaces) project from the Interreg Danube Region. The conference aimed to bring together key stakeholders from local, regional, national, and international levels to foster dialogue on sustainable development in industrial and rural areas. Participants explored best practices through presentations, thematic site visits, and discussions focused on green investments, cultural heritage revitalization, and urban transformation projects, aligning with the goals of a green and just transition. The conference was covered by multiple newspapers, television channels, and other news outlets.

By the end of December 2024, data was collected from partners regarding pilot areas to be included in the GJT Dashboard.

Communication was maintained through online meetings, email exchanges, and an in person meeting. The LP also participated in the MED Talks (held on September 9, 2024, and December 16, 2024), which were organized by the programme to engage with other projects and stakeholders in the region.

Up to this period, all tasks were successfully conducted. During this reporting period, the BauNow project achieved important progress across  communication and pilot implementation. 

Collaboration in project was strengthened through online and in-person meetings. An online project meeting took place on 20 May 2025, complemented by several bilateral sessions with pilot partners.

GI updated the project website with detailed news on pilot progress and major events, while the project’s Facebook channel was managed as a tool for wider visibility. Communication deliverables included monthly outreach reports to monitor partners. 

BauNow also gained visibility through participation in several major events. LP represented the project at the Interreg Euro-MED Green Living Areas Mission event in Barcelona on 28 May 2025. Slovenian partners organized the international capitalization event Inclusive Tourism as a Development and Investment Opportunity in the Danube Region on 2–3 June 2025 in Moravske Toplice. The event gathered multiple Interreg projects and hosted a BauNow information point. At national level, Slovenian, Croatian, and Albanian partners joined Green Living Areas mission events in April 2025, where they presented BauNow results and explored synergies with other initiatives.

Important progress was also made on the common BauNow methodology, which continues to be refined through monitoring and evaluation of pilot cases, coordination meetings, and field visits. GI, REAN, and Iskriva advanced work on the Green and Just Transition (GJT) Dashboard, holding technical meetings to review functionalities and alignment with project goals. The Dashboard is being designed as a practical online tool to guide public authorities through all stages of the project lifecycle, from planning to financing and implementation.

Pilot cases across partner countries demonstrated concrete achievements:
-	Croatia: Partners organized three workshops from March to June 25: Solar Potential Map to stakeholders, Financial instruments for household solar power plants, Municipal financing models for GJT measures. REA North further developed VIREAS, an AI-based chatbot providing personalized GJT information to citizens. Extensive testing and optimization were conducted during early 2025. VIREAS now integrates a technical library of guidelines and regulations, public co-financing schemes, and energy methodologies. It is accompanied by a user manual, a short citizen guide, and a one-pager for advanced users, making it fully operational and scalable.
-	France: Geres supported Aix-Marseille-Provence Métropole in establishing a cooperative financing structure for GJT projects, with a strong focus on governance and transparency in carbon financing. Geres prepared terms of reference for a study on the drivers of demand for local carbon projects, selected a consultant, and attended a high-profile carbon finance event in London on 3 June 2025. It also completed the internal process to become a founding member of the cooperative, ensuring long-term involvement.
-	Bosnia and Herzegovina: The City of Cazin progressed with solar energy cooperatives. Technical documentation for photovoltaic power plants on four public buildings was prepared, representing a significant step toward pilot implementation.
-	Montenegro: UDG launched its roadshow activities. The first event, the Entrepreneurial Ideas Market in Podgorica, selected two startups for continued participation. The initiative also built momentum for creating a community of investors and practitioners supporting green and just transition solutions.
-	Albania: AUT concentrated on Dajti National Park, where it secured funding for bicycles promoting green mobility, prepared investments for solar panels at the Visitor Center, and explored opportunities for solar-powered benches. A stakeholder questionnaire on renewable energy and green transition was developed for distribution in the coming months.
-	Italy: Kyoto club organised the first Green and Just Transition Hackathon, held in Bologna on 26 June 2025. This Italian pilot case brought together the Bologna Municipality, the Emilia-Romagna Regional Government, and local stakeholders. The Hackathon aligned with the Build Better Lives campaign, produced valuable inputs for the BauNow Dashboard, and generated concrete recommendations for local and regional authorities. It also strengthened connections with the broader GJT community, setting an excellent example of participatory innovation.
-	Slovenia: GI held several meetings with the Municipality of Kočevje to identify challenges and needs related to e-mobility and the purchase of e-cars.

Peer learning remained a key component. During a site visit in Cazin (February 25), LP GI and Iskriva got familiar with the Cazin pilot case. This exchange promoted mutual learning, provided insights into local contexts, and encouraged on-site discussions about improvements and future opportunities.

fr.name: Investing in Green and Just Transition for a New Sustainable Business as Usual Economy
fr.description:
Les zones urbaines et urbaines-rurales de la région MED sont menacées par des impacts négatifs du changement climatique et des pratiques de développement non durables. Cela demande un changement d'une ancienne à une nouvelle économie - des nouveaux modèles de consommation, d'entreprise et de financement, portant à une économie “Business as usual” (BAU) nouvelle, économe en ressources, et une transition verte et équitable (TVE).
Le projet BauNOW vise d'améliorer la planification et le financement des mesures de la TVE auprès des acteurs publics et privés vers les zones de vie climatiquement neutres résilientes. Avec des nouvelles pratiques de développement intégrées et un meilleur accès au financement, les secteurs public et privé dans les domaines des projets MED reconnaîtront le potentiel commercial et de développement de la transition, ce qui en fera leur nouveau “BAU”.
Le tableau de bord pour la TVE -un simple outil de projets web- peut faciliter cela, offrant une méthodologie ascendante pour concevoir, financer et mettre en œuvre la TVE locale ou régionale. Il servira les autorités publiques, les praticiens et le secteur privé et sera validé par des processus horizontaux dans 9 zones pilotes dans 7 pays MED, conduisant à des mesures concrètes. Le plan de transférabilité, visé sur l'accès aux investissements publics et privés, le transférera à d'autres régions MED et au-delà, par le biais d'une communauté de praticiens, d'investissement et des partenariats.
Les régions et les partenaires représentent la diversité de l'espace MED et visent à combler ses disparités régionales par le développement conjoint de solutions efficaces et transférables.
BauNOW, une nouvelle perspective sur le développement, fournit un outil pour un changement de paradigme dans la planification du développement, au-delà des approches sectorielles, ouvrant des opportunités pour que les secteurs public et privé réévaluent les principes d'investissement et reconnaissent le potentiel de la TVE.

en.name: MEDiterranean COllaborative LOgistics for the URban Space
en.description: Climate change is the foremost worldwide challenge. Some of the most pressing factors include congestion, which costs the European economy €100 billion per year- 1% of its GDP, air pollution, affecting people’s health, and transport emissions (freight counts 25% of total urban transport emissions). The EU Green Deal targets to reduce emissions in cities by 55% by 2030 and be climate-neutral by 2050 cannot be met only by vehicles decarbonisation goals. MED COLOURS aims at upscaling to a new generation of urban logistics and planning enabling the transition to decarbonised and smart cities. By developing new Sustainable Urban Logistics Plans (SULPs), MED Cities will plan resilient, sustainable, integrated, and collaborative innovation-driven solutions for the Functional Urban Areas (FUAs). This would help reducing the negative impacts of freight and logistics activities. MED COLOURS’ joint territorial ambition is to enable urban freight distribution in MED-FUAs from a low carbon environmental perspective and, at the same time, give cities a boost in sustainable development and human well-being. MED COLOURS will jointly validate 1) strategy for continuous cross-monitoring of SULPs 2) New Resilient SULPs and 3) solutions for innovative and sustainable urban logistics. This would foster the more effective implementation of existing sustainable policies and improve access to funds for climate-resilient living areas. MED COLOURS supports the digital and environmental transition in six MED cities (Livorno, Cesena, Thessaloniki, Koper, Lisbon, and Lyon) and their related FUAs . Transition is built upon a quadruple helix approach supported by the liaison with other European Projects in the same field. MED COLOURS will adopt a new planning approach introducing new collaborative concepts and disruptive/enabling technologies. The project outcomes will go beyond existing practices through long-term transition scenarios to achieve the targets set in the EU green Deal in MED Area.
en.expected_achievements:
Set up a methodological and operational framework for the development of resilient sustainable urban logistics plans based on collaborative paradigms and open to innovation
WP2  validates sustainable efficient urban logistics solutions in real conditions responding to specific and common territorial needs, ready for take-up, upscale and transfer in MED COLOURS living areas and beyond.  
Transnational cooperation to develop synergies and capacities supporting the urban transition to carbon neutral logistics according to operational scenarios of resilience, collaboration and innovation.
en.actual_achievements:
Overall, the implementation of MED COLOURS activities proceed according to schedule. After a slow start due to the articulated contracting phase, activities kicked off smoothly with a Virtual Kick-off meeting held online on March 20th 2024. During Period 1 activities concentrated particularly on Activity 1.1 "Legacy, capitalisation and adaptation of background knowledge/experiences to SULPs/needs of COLOURS urban areas", Activity 3.1 "Coordination with MED TCP and other EU Projects for building synergies", Activity 3.2 "Coordination with IDP and other Initiatives for capacity development", Activity 3.3 "Monitoring of COLOURS impacts". 
In the context of A.1.1 project partners produced D.1.1.1 "COLOURS SULPs benchmark".	
The MED COLOURS project has made significant progress across multiple work packages, achieving key objectives and delivering impactful outputs that contribute to sustainable urban logistics in MED COLOURS cities (Livorno, Cesena, Thessaloniki, Koper, Lisbon, and Lyon). 

In Period 1, in the context of WP1, Activity A1.1 produced an initial benchmark study comparing urban logistics policies and needs of MED COLOURS cities with best practices derived from major EU projects and initiatives. This research established a foundational understanding of the current context and successful practices, which informed the development of a common framework for sustainable and resilient urban logistics planning. By Period 2, the framework design entered its core phase under A1.2, laying the groundwork for a harmonized approach among partner cities towards joint methodologies and tools for sustainable resilient urban logistics planning.

Activity A2.1 launched during Period 2, led by ZLC with support from ITL, focused on feasibility studies for the pilot actions. Collaborative efforts between pilot cities and knowledge partners evaluated the financial, technical, social, and policy feasibility of the planned pilot activities to be implemented in Periods 3 and 4 (A2.2). This phase ensured that pilot actions are well-prepared, actionable, and tailored to local contexts while maintaining alignment with overarching project goals.

In the context of WP3, the MED COLOURS consortium set the basis for a strong engagement with Green Living Areas Mission and other EU-funded projects. The consortium established meaningful collaborations with the Green Living Areas (GLA) Mission and its governance projects, fostering synergies and knowledge exchange. Representatives from GLA were invited to the MED COLOURS Bologna meeting (June 5-6, 2024), facilitating direct interaction and collaboration. MED COLOURS partners actively participated in key GLA Mission events, including in-presence events such as the kick-off meeting in Turin (April 2024) and the Green Mobility thematic working group (June 2024). External visibility was further strengthened by presenting MED COLOURS at international events such as the 8th Danube Region Transport Days 2024 and the Urban Logistics Innovation Day 2024 in Brussels. Further, the consortium mapped and initiated collaboration with relevant projects to enhance the project’s impact and scalability. Regular engagement on platforms such as Basecamp ensured continuous dialogue and updates with other EU projects, fostering a dynamic exchange of ideas and best practices.

The cooperative nature of MED COLOURS has been instrumental in knowldge sharing and cross-city learning. Collaboration among partner cities has fostered the adaptation of best practices to diverse local context. Close cooperation with knowledge partners like ZLC, ITL, Umaribor, MemEx, CERTH, and Lisboa E-Nova has facilitated the informed design of pilot actions and will be increasingly valuable throught the project implementation. Indeed, active participation in EU-level initiatives has positioned MED COLOURS as a key contributor to sustainable urban logistics discussions.

In short, MED COLOURS has made significant strides in benchmarking, framework development, feasibility assessments, and stakeholder engagement. The project’s achievements reflect a shared commitment to fostering sustainable and resilient urban logistics across MED COLOURS cities, creating a model for replicability and long-term impact.

Overall, the implementation of MED COLOURS activities proceeds according to schedule. The engagement levels of the project partners is high and a collaborative approach is fostered by the LP throught the regular monthly meetings and the WP coordination meetings. The MED COLOURS Consortium Meeting & Steering Committee held in Lyon on the 15th and 16th May 2025 marked a significant mid-term milestone, renewing PPs energies and commitment to the project. Significant progress has been made in the development of joint methodologies and tools for sustainable resilient urban logistics planning, thanks to the leadership of PP3 CERTH and the other knowledge partners of the consortium. Following the fine-tuninig of the feasibility study and preparatory activities, pilot activities have been clearly defined (and pilot factsheets produced for each city) and works underway. Under the leadership of PP6 MemEx, a monitoring plan has been developed and implemented to ensure a smooth implementation of the solutions and the collection of data, organizing also a risk analysis and two rounds of peer-review workshops which each city and knowledge partners. Finally,  the MED COLOURS Consortium has been very active in creating synergies with other projects and promoting institutional dialogue. The Consortium has joined the ALICE Liason Programme, which provides support to project dissemination through ALICE communication channels and grant free access for MED COLOURS partners to ​the ALICE Knowledge Platform. In the context of WP3, channels of communication and exchanges have been opened with other urban logistics projects and stakeholders on innovative solutions, enabling technologies, future trends in urban logistics.

fr.description:
Le changement climatique est le défi mondial n°1. La congestion coûte à l'économie européenne 100 milliards€/an, 1 % de son PIB. La pollution atmosphérique affecte la santé. Le fret urbain représente 25 % des émissions totales du transport urbain. Les objectifs du Green Deal pour les villes (55% de réduction des émissions d’ici 2030, neutralité climatique en 2050), ne peuvent être atteints uniquement par la décarbonisation des véhicules.
MED COLOURS vise à une nouvelle génération de logistique urbaine et de planification intelligente pour des villes décarbonées et numériques. Les nouveaux Plan de logistique urbaine durable (PLUD) serviront la réduction de l'impact du fret , permettant aux Zones Urbaines Fonctionnelles (ZUF) MED de planifier des solutions innovantes, résilientes, durables, intégrées et collaboratives. Le défi territorial de MED COLOURS est de proposer des solutions de fret urbain bas carbone dans les ZUF, tout en assurant aux villes un rôle vital pour stimuler le développement durable et la gestion des espaces de vie.
MED COLOURS développera 1) une stratégie de suivi croisé continu des PLUD favorisant une instauration plus efficace des politiques existantes et améliorant l'accès au fonds pour des espaces de vie plus résilients 2) six PLUD résilients 3) des solutions innovantes, durables de logistique urbaine.
MED COLOURS soutient la transition numérique et environnementale dans six villes MED et leurs ZUF (Cesena, Koper, Lisbonne, Livourne, Lyon, Thessalonique). La transition repose sur une approche en quadruple hélice et sur la liaison avec d'autres projets européens similaires.
MED COLOURS abordera la planification du fret urbain via de nouveaux concepts collaboratifs et l'introduction de nouvelles technologies, allant au-delà des pratiques existantes vers une planification verte et cohérente avec des scénarios résilients à long terme de transition urbaine pour atteindre les objectifs du Green Deal dans la zone MED.

en.name: Greener AgRo-fooD logistics in the mEditerraneaN area
en.description: The project aims to make more ecological logistics for food supply and the management of bio-waste, packaging, and used containers in Mediterranean cities, in order to reduce their impact on citizens and the global climate system. To do this, 6 pilot demonstrators will be set up in Spain, France, Italy, Greece, and Bulgaria, which will support experiments for the ecological transition. Modeling and sizing work on suitable infrastructures, based on renewable energy (eg: charging stations with photovoltaic panels) and bio-fuels should make it possible to showcase the feasibility of carbon-free alternative solutions. In addition, to consider the transition in a long-term approach, the consortium will address the economic, organizational, and technological issues associated with the imperative of energy transition. Through a multi-actor consultation in a living lab approach within the demonstrators on the pilot territories, the consortium will identify levers to make viable and secure the entire supply chain from producer to consumer by effectively connecting the different operators involved. The results of these experiments will demonstrate the possibility to formulate recommendations for decision-makers to transform the city (urban development, provision of infrastructures) in order to face the challenges of tomorrow. The project consortium includes 10 beneficiary partners and 9 associated partners, public or private, skilled in logistics in territorial food systems, design of carbon-free logistics solutions, and support for public energy transition policies.
